metadata.contributionModel: Public GitHub pull requests against a single curriculum JSON

tracks.title: Computer Science Foundations
tracks.tagline: Open-source mastery learning for computational thinking.
tracks.description: An initial open computer science curriculum with a prerequisite graph, lesson checks, reviews, and local mastery state.

courses.title: Computational Thinking Core
courses.tagline: From program flow to conditions, loops, and debugging.
courses.description: This sample course shows how open content can later be extended by the community along a shared skill graph.

modules.title: Program State
modules.summary: Learn how programs change state step by step.

modules.title: Control Flow
modules.summary: Understand how conditions and loops control program flow.

modules.title: Debugging Mindset
modules.summary: Build a reproducible debugging process instead of guessing.

skills.title: Read sequential program flow
skills.description: Can trace a linear program flow step by step.

skills.title: Track variable state
skills.description: Can determine the current value of a variable after multiple assignments.

skills.title: Evaluate boolean branches
skills.description: Can predict which branch of an if/else decision will execute.

skills.title: Trace simple loops
skills.description: Can determine loop iterations and final state in simple examples.

skills.title: Isolate bugs with hypotheses
skills.description: Can approach a bug with reproduce-isolate-inspect instead of guessing.

lessons.title: Read sequential programs
lessons.summary: Programs do not run magically. They execute in a precisely defined order.
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you will be able to read a linear flow and separate input, processing, and output.

sections.title: Programs are executable instructions
body: A computer does not interpret intent. It executes exactly the instructions written in the program.
When reading code, a simple lens helps: what comes in, what changes, and what comes out at the end?
sections.checkpoint: Why can swapping just two lines completely change the output?

workedExamples.prompt: read input -> double it -> output result

lessons.title: Track variable state
lessons.summary: Variables store state. Strong programmers read assignments as state transformations.
lessons.objective: You can determine the final value of a variable after multiple updates.

sections.title: Assignment changes state
body: In `score = score + 1`, you are not doing algebra. You are transforming state.
Read every assignment as: retrieve the old value, perform an operation, store the new value.
sections.checkpoint: Which two pieces of information do you need to predict the final variable value?

lessons.title: Boolean branches
lessons.summary: Conditions split program flow into possible paths.
lessons.objective: You can predict which branch of an if/else structure will execute.

sections.title: Conditions are decisions
body: A condition evaluates an expression to `true` or `false`.
After that, exactly the branch whose condition is satisfied continues running.
sections.checkpoint: Why are conditions the basis of reactive program behavior?

workedExamples.prompt: if temperature > 30 then warm else cool
steps: Determine the true or false branch.

lessons.title: Trace loops
lessons.summary: Loops compress repetition. The key skill is simulating iterations carefully.
lessons.objective: You can simulate simple loops by hand and determine the final state.

sections.title: Loops are structured repetition
body: Loops replace copy-paste with repeated execution under clear rules.
When tracing, it helps to write down the counter and every changed variable for each iteration.
sections.checkpoint: What should you write down for each iteration so you do not lose track of the loop?

workedExamples.prompt: sum = 0; for i in [1,2,3]: sum = sum + i

lessons.title: Debugging with hypotheses
lessons.summary: Good debugging is structured investigation, not frantic rewriting.
lessons.objective: You can approach a bug systematically with reproduce, isolate, inspect, and verify.

sections.title: From guesses to testable steps
body: Instead of changing code at random, form a hypothesis about the cause of the bug.
Then reduce uncertainty: reproduce, isolate, inspect state, fix, verify.
sections.checkpoint: Why is isolating a bug often more effective than immediately rewriting code?

workedExamples.prompt: A counter ends at 1 instead of 3.
steps: Log the state after each change.
Narrow the mismatch down to a specific line.

assessments.title: Entry diagnostic
assessments.description: Determines which core skills already exist and which ones should be practiced first.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes sequential program flow?
options: The computer randomly picks a suitable line
The computer executes instructions in a defined order
The computer only executes the last line
items.correctAnswer: The computer executes instructions in a defined order
items.explanation: A sequential program follows a fixed sequence of steps.

items.prompt: `x = 2`; then `x = x + 5`. What is the final value of `x`?
items.explanation: The old value 2 is read, 5 is added, and the result is stored again.

items.prompt: If `score = 12` and the condition is `score > 10`, which branch runs?
`sum = 0`; then a loop adds `1`, `2`, and `3`. What is the final value of `sum`?
What is the best first step in debugging?
options: Reproduce the bug and inspect state
items.correctAnswer: Reproduce the bug and inspect state
items.explanation: Without a reproducible bug, there is no solid basis for targeted analysis.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Read sequential programs
assessments.description: Checks whether a linear flow can be read step by step.

items.prompt: Which step comes first in a simple program flow?
items.explanation: Without input or an initial state, no processing can happen yet.

items.prompt: Put the steps of a simple flow in order.
items.explanation: The standard pattern is input, processing, output.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Track variable state
assessments.description: Checks whether assignments can be read as state changes.

items.prompt: `coins = 4`; then `coins = coins + 2`. What is the final value?
items.explanation: 4 is read, 2 is added, and the result is stored.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly describes `score = score + 1`?
items.explanation: The existing value is read, changed, and stored again.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Boolean branches
assessments.description: Checks whether conditions lead to the correct branch.

items.prompt: If `temperature = 31` and `temperature > 30`, which branch runs?
A boolean expression always evaluates to `true` or ...
items.explanation: Boolean expressions have exactly two possible outcomes.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Trace loops
assessments.description: Checks whether simple repetition can be simulated.

items.prompt: `count = 0`; a loop increments `count` by 1 three times. What is the final value?
Which note helps most when tracing a loop?
options: Write down only the first line
Record the counter and important variables for each iteration
items.correctAnswer: Record the counter and important variables for each iteration
items.explanation: That keeps the changing state visible from one round to the next.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Debugging with hypotheses
assessments.description: Checks whether a structured debugging workflow is understood.

items.prompt: Which step comes before fixing a bug?
items.explanation: Before fixing anything, it must be clear where and how the bug appears.

items.prompt: Put the debugging workflow in order.
items.explanation: A clear workflow reduces random action and creates evidence.

assessments.title: Review: Read sequential flow
assessments.description: Short review for the core skill of linear program flow.

items.prompt: Which pattern is fundamental in many programs?
items.explanation: This basic pattern remains visible even in more complex programs.

items.prompt: `points = 10`; then `points = points - 4`. Final value?
A condition evaluates to `true` or ...

assessments.title: Review: Trace loops
assessments.description: Short review for simple loop simulation.

items.prompt: What is tracing used for in loops?
items.explanation: Tracing reduces complexity to controlled intermediate steps.

items.prompt: What reduces debugging uncertainty most effectively?
options: Forming a hypothesis and testing it directly
items.correctAnswer: Forming a hypothesis and testing it directly
items.explanation: Systematic testing replaces guessing with evidence.
